comments.text: A small government-funded program has reduced the sea lamprey population in the Great Lakes by more than 90 per cent, saving the region’s $6 billion fishing industry. Now the program is at risk.

While this is good news, the #GreatLakes lamprey control program is now several weeks behind schedule for this year's treatment, and it’s still unclear how many of the previously-fired workers will actually choose to return www.michiganpublic.org/environment-...

We spoke up and fought back. Now critical programs are being reinstated. The Trump Administration this week reversed its freeze on staffing and funding the Great Lakes Sea Lamprey Control Program. When we hold folks accountable, we get results.

It is tough to do sea lamprey control in the US waters of the Great Lakes when front line control agents are fired. Just one of the consequences associated with random terminations of environmental professionals in the US.
www.bridgemi.com/michigan-env...

Cuts to the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service have slashed the Great Lakes sea lamprey control program. The remaining staff are left scrambling to ensure that populations of the invasive eel don’t skyrocket as a result.
Sea Lamprey control reduction during the COVID-19 pandemic corresponds to rapid increase in Sea Lamprey abundance #GreatLakes 🧪 academic.oup.com/fisheries/ad...

Any reduction in sea lamprey control will devastate Great Lakes Fisheries! Hopefully, this move is similar to the "fire the guys who maintain the nukes and then re-hire them" sort of thing...but somehow I doubt Trump cares about fisheries. www.greatlakesnow.org/2025/02/trum...

lol, the administration who fired all the sea lamprey control workers suddenly gives a shit about Asian Carp and the Great Lakes? fuck offfff lol

Oh it's not the lamprey that will be the problem. They also fired the USACE people holding back the asian carp at Chicago. Once that fails all the Great Lakes will suffer a shock way bigger than the lamprey and zebra mussels combined. icrcc.fws.gov

Saving the Great Lakes from being destroyed by algae blooms and invasive lampreys requires US-Canada cooperation. The commercial fishery depends on it. This documentary explores the fishers who work those waters. www.cbc.ca/news/canada/...

Reduced Sea Lamprey control efforts during the 2020 Covid shutdown caused an increase in #GreatLakes lamprey numbers. But control efforts since then have already brought them back under control. This demonstrates the importance of maintaining control efforts.
www.michiganpublic.org/environment-...

As someone who grew up on Lake Michigan, I know how important it is to keep the sea lampreys under control. Defunding of the lamprey-control program will impact sport fishing, tourism, and sustinance fishing throughout the entire Great Lakes region.
Great Lakes fish at risk as sea lamprey control faces layoffs

My partner and I watched this movie thinking his grandpa, Vernon Applegate, was mentioned. Turns out it’s largely about his work ridding the Great Lakes of sea lamprey. Since Trump fired 14 federal workers involved in the lamprey control program, we’re going to see a lot more lamprey and alewives.
Hydraulic characteristics of effective sea lamprey barriers #GreatLakes www.sciencedirect.com/science/arti...
Project 2025 = 450 billion lamprey eggs in the Great Lakes 🤯
THIS is an emergency. The lamprey have no natural predators in the Great Lakes. Until the chemical was found it control them, they decimated lake trout. The control program costs $20 million a year.
New trap is the only indexing site for #sea #lamprey on Canadian side of Lake Superior www.cbc.ca/news/canada/... via .ca

How an eel-like bloodsucking invasive species nearly wiped out fishing in the Great Lakes
Lampreys have been under control for decades, but ravaged fish populations before then www.cbc.ca/news/canada/...

Sea lamprey control - a joint effort between the US + Canada - has allowed lake trout to make a comeback in the #GreatLakes over the past 60 years. This fall marks the 3rd year the Fond du Lac Band has been able to practice the tradition of subsistence netting www.mprnews.org/story/2025/1...

Sea lamprey are not supposed to be in the Great Lakes, which is why the U.S. spends about $20 million each year to protect the multi-billion dollar fisheries that lamprey threaten. That budget has remained at around $20 million since 2010.

We’ve been doing a great job at getting sea lamprey under control in the Great Lakes…but it’s an effort that has to be done continuously, or the invasive parasite will rebound. Indiscriminately cutting jobs and funding leads to bad consequences - in this case destruction of fisheries.
HOAs are the lamprey in the great lakes of garage bands.
they are still edible: "there had been discussion of capturing Great Lakes sea lamprey for human consumption, it was ultimately determined not to be the most effective way to control the population"

And now I'm doubly pissed because after all that has been done to save the Great Lakes his cuts are going end the Sea Lamprey control project and the fish and all those industries tied to those fish will perish.

the symbolism is sadly ironic too. bringing the lamprey under control was a huge victory and a landmark achievement for the joint commissions that Canada and the US formed to address problems in the Great Lakes. a real show of what we can get done by working together

All ten, but they're shorter. Seriously, it was a freakish phenomenon but all part of a unique environment, northern Lake Michigan before the invasion of the zebra mussel but after the alewife brought under control by the salmon introduced to make up for the lake trout lost to the lamprey invasion.

That lamprey eel problem has plagued the Great Lakes since I was a kid. It’s important that this program to control them should remain fully funded.

Sea lamprey control efforts are largely being carried out by the Great Lakes Fishery Commission, a joint effort supported through the Fisheries and Oceans Canada and the US Fish and Wildlife Service. Just another example of the impact destroying friendly relations with our neighbors will have

When feeding on a host, they release lamphredin, which stops their host's blood from clotting. In the Great Lakes, where fish haven't evolved defenses against this, the host often dies of blood loss or infection. There are several control efforts being used now, with varying levels of success
3/4
